posts.raw_content:
Great example is the Ford auto plant in the Soviet Union following the communist takeover.  Operations and productivity quickly plummeted after Ford engineers left (they had a 5 year deal).  It was blamed on "decay" or "wreckers."

Henry Ford had sent a delegation in 1926 looking to open a factory there, but found Soviet industry languishing in state of chaos. Coupled with the fear of government takeover, he didn't build. In 1929, Ford sold the Soviets an old Model A plant that he was going to scrap, with the Russians hoping to build a "Soviet Detroit."   In 1932, Soviets finished the plant, partially made of bricks from destroyed churches.  A number of Americans who left the US in search of a "workers paradise" worked at the plant, but most "disappeared " by the mid-30s.

In fact, nearly every Soviet engineer who worked with the Americans to start the plant were also sent to the Gulags. When things went south after the American engineers left, they were accused of being "wreckers."   The first plant director, initially called the "Soviet Ford" was arrested and shot in Lubyanka prison on the day of his trial.

The Soviets were given the machinery, blueprints, examples, and even knowledgeable workers, but their system turned it to shit.

A really great read on the plight of Americans who went to the Soviet Union during the depression is "The Forsaken" by Tzouliadis (spelling?).  Highly recommend. Book also destroys Duranty and the New York Times for being the complete excrement they are.
